children.name: Melanocharis
children.description: The typical berrypeckers — a genus of small, fruit-eating passerines endemic to New Guinea, with short tails and fine, slightly curved bills. Their plumage is sexually dimorphic: males are glossy blue-black or olive above, yellow or white below, while females are duller. They inhabit montane and submontane forests, where they feed primarily on small fruits and berries.

children.name: Melanocharis nigra
children.description: A small berrypecker with the male glossy blue-black above and white below, widespread across the lowlands and hills of New Guinea and nearby islands. It is the most common and widespread melanocharitid, found from sea level to 1,800 metres in forest edge and secondary growth. It feeds on small fruits and occasionally insects.

children.name: Melanocharis versteri
children.description: A montane berrypecker with a distinctive white-tipped, fan-shaped tail and the male a striking grey-black with a yellow belly, found in New Guinea from 1,400 to 3,000 metres. It forages actively in the lower canopy and understorey, taking small fruits and berries. Its call is a thin, high-pitched whistle.

children.name: Melanocharis longicauda
children.description: A medium-sized berrypecker with a relatively long tail, found in the mid-montane forests of New Guinea between 1,200 and 2,100 metres. The male has glossy blue-black upperparts and a yellow belly, while the female is olive-brown above and yellowish below. It feeds on small fruits and berries in the forest understorey.

children.name: Melanocharis arfakiana
children.description: A poorly known berrypecker from the Arfak Mountains of western New Guinea, with sooty-black plumage in males and dull olive-brown in females. It is one of the least studied members of the family, known from only a handful of specimens. It inhabits montane forests above 1,500 metres.

children.name: Melanocharis striativentris
children.description: A distinctive berrypecker with heavily streaked white underparts and olive-brown upperparts, found in the montane forests of New Guinea. It has a relatively long, slightly decurved bill adapted for probing into flowers and crevices for insects and fruit. It forages actively in the lower canopy and understorey.

children.name: Rhamphocharis
children.description: A small genus of berrypeckers with notably thick, deep bills adapted for crushing seeds and hard fruits, endemic to the mountain forests of New Guinea. They are medium-sized by melanocharitid standards, with spotted underparts that provide good camouflage in the forest gloom.

children.name: Rhamphocharis crassirostris
children.description: A sturdy, thick-billed berrypecker with olive-green upperparts heavily spotted with white and a whitish belly, found in montane forests of New Guinea from 1,200 to 3,300 metres. Its stout bill allows it to feed on hard seeds and fruits that are unavailable to other berrypeckers.

children.name: Toxorhamphus
children.description: Small, long-billed berrypeckers found in New Guinea, closely related to the true berrypeckers. They have relatively long, slender bills adapted for feeding on nectar and small fruits, filling an ecological niche similar to sunbirds.

children.name: Toxorhamphus novaeguineae
children.description: A small, active bird with olive-green upperparts, bright yellow underparts, and a relatively long, decurved bill, found across New Guinea in lowland and hill forests. It feeds on nectar and small fruits, often hovering briefly at flowers like a sunbird. Its call is a sharp, high-pitched note.

children.name: Toxorhamphus poliopterus
children.description: A distinctive longbill with a grey head, olive-green back, and yellow underparts, found in the montane forests of New Guinea. It has a relatively long, decurved bill used for feeding on nectar and small berries. It inhabits mossy cloud forests at elevations between 1,500 and 3,000 metres.

children.name: Oedistoma
children.description: Tiny, long-billed birds from New Guinea, among the smallest members of the berrypecker family. They have fine, decurved bills adapted for feeding on nectar and small insects, and were once considered part of the flowerpecker family.

children.name: Oedistoma iliolophus
children.description: A tiny bird with olive-green upperparts, pale yellowish underparts, and a fine decurved bill, found in lowland and hill forests across New Guinea. It feeds on nectar and small insects, often in mixed-species flocks. It is one of the smallest members of the berrypecker family, measuring only 8 cm in length.

children.name: Oedistoma pygmaeum
children.description: One of the smallest birds in New Guinea, with olive-green upperparts, pale grey underparts, and a very fine, decurved bill. It inhabits lowland and hill forests, feeding on nectar and small insects. It is an active, acrobatic forager that often hovers briefly while feeding.

children.name: Oedistoma spectaculare
children.description: A tiny longbill with a distinctive white eye-ring (spectacles), olive-green upperparts, and yellowish underparts, found in the montane forests of New Guinea. It has a fine decurved bill used for feeding on nectar and small insects. It is an active, acrobatic forager, often hovering at flowers.
